content:
# Mark Chapter 7 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 35v–36r (PDF pages 70–71) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_007_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_007_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ The Pharisees and some of the scribes who had come from Jerusalem gathered around him.

² They noticed that some of his disciples ate with defiled—that is, unwashed—hands.

³ (For the Pharisees, and all the Jews, do not eat unless they wash their hands carefully, holding to the tradition of the elders,

⁴ and when they come from the marketplace, they do not eat unless they wash. They observe many other traditions as well, such as the washing of cups, pitchers, and copper vessels.)

⁵ The Pharisees and scribes asked him, "Why do your disciples not follow the tradition of the elders, but eat with defiled hands?"

⁶ He said to them, "Well did Isaiah prophesy about you hypocrites, as it is written: 'This people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me.

⁷ In vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men.'

⁸ You abandon the commandment of God and hold to human tradition."

⁹ And he said to them, "You have a fine way of setting aside God's commandment in order to keep your own tradition!

¹⁰ For Moses said, 'Honor your father and your mother,' and, 'Whoever speaks evil of father or mother must be put to death.'

¹¹ But you say, 'If someone tells his father or mother, "Whatever you might have received from me is Corban"'—that is, a gift devoted to God—

¹² then you no longer permit him to do anything for his father or mother,

¹³ making void the word of God by the tradition you have handed down. And you do many other things like this."

¹⁴ He called the crowd to him again and said, "Listen to me, all of you, and understand:

¹⁵ Nothing that goes into a person from outside can defile them. It is what comes out of a person that defiles them."

¹⁶ [If anyone has ears to hear, let him hear.]

¹⁷ When he had left the crowd and gone into the house, his disciples asked him about the parable.

¹⁸ He said to them, "Are you also without understanding? Do you not see that whatever goes into a person from outside cannot defile them,

¹⁹ since it enters not the heart but the stomach, and passes out of the body?" (By saying this, he declared all foods clean.)

²⁰ He said, "What comes out of a person is what defiles them.

²¹ For from within, out of the human heart, come evil thoughts: sexual immorality, theft, murder,

²² adultery, greed, wickedness, deceit, indulgence, envy, slander, pride, foolishness.

²³ All these evils come from within, and they defile a person."

²⁴ He left that place and went to the region of Tyre. He entered a house and did not want anyone to know he was there, but he could not remain hidden.

²⁵ Immediately a woman whose little daughter had an unclean spirit heard about him and came and fell at his feet.

²⁶ The woman was a Greek, a Syrophoenician by birth. She begged him to cast the demon out of her daughter.

²⁷ He said to her, "Let the children be fed first, for it is not right to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs."

²⁸ But she answered him, "Lord, even the dogs under the table eat the children's crumbs."

²⁹ He said to her, "For saying that, go—the demon has left your daughter."

³⁰ She went home and found the child lying on the bed, and the demon gone.

³¹ Then he returned from the region of Tyre and went through Sidon to the Sea of Galilee, through the region of the Decapolis.

³² They brought to him a man who was deaf and could hardly speak, and they begged him to lay his hand on him.

³³ Taking him aside from the crowd, Jesus put his fingers into the man's ears and, after spitting, touched his tongue.

³⁴ Looking up to heaven, he sighed deeply and said to him, "*Ephphatha*"—that is, "Be opened."

³⁵ And his ears were opened, his tongue was released, and he began to speak clearly.

³⁶ He ordered them to tell no one, but the more he ordered them, the more eagerly they proclaimed it.

³⁷ They were utterly astonished, saying, "He has done all things well. He makes even the deaf to hear and the mute to speak."

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

1. **v. 16 — Verse inclusion:** Sinaiticus appears to include this verse; Vaticanus omits it. EMET brackets it. LOGOS brackets it. PNEUMA brackets it. I bracket it as textually uncertain but present in Sinaiticus.

2. **v. 19 — "He declared all foods clean":** All three translators preserve this crucial editorial parenthesis. PNEUMA's note about whether this is Jesus's intention or Mark's retroactive interpretation is a fair observation, but the text as we have it presents this as Mark's authoritative gloss on Jesus's teaching.

3. **v. 27 — "dogs" (κυνάρια):** EMET: "little-dogs." LOGOS: "dogs." PNEUMA: "dogs." I adopted "dogs" — while the Greek uses the diminutive, the diminutive does not significantly soften the metaphor. The exchange's power lies in the woman's wit, not in Jesus's courtesy.

4. **v. 9 — Ironic ΚΑΛΩΣ:** EMET: "Well do you set aside." LOGOS: "You have a fine way of setting aside." PNEUMA: "You have quite a talent for sidestepping." All three capture the sarcasm. I followed LOGOS—clear, formal, with just enough edge.

5. **Gentile mission theme:** All three translators note the geographical progression through Gentile territory (Tyre, Sidon, Decapolis) and the healing of a Gentile woman's daughter. This section of Mark prepares for the universal scope of the gospel.

No ⚠️ flagged disagreements for this chapter.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
